observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary disclosures required by the relevant IPSASs and the NGAS Manual for LGUs particularly the assumption and estimates used in recognizing the Impairment Loss - Lease Receivable account and/or the events and circumstances leading to the impairment loss.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management thru the Municipal Accountant monitor and issue demand letters to the concerned officials and employees to fully liquidate their cash advances, otherwise withhold the salaries of the erring employees, if warranted, as provided under COA Circular 2012-004 dated November 28, 2012. Moreover, we recommended that all cash advances be fully liquidated at the end of each year.

We recommended that Management consider transferring the PPEs to the recipient offices by accomplishing the Property Transfer Receipts (PTRs) which will be the basis of Municipal Accountant in preparing the following journal entries to drop the items of PPE from the books of accounts of LGU San Mateo, Isabela to come up with an accurate and reliable financial information.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant recognize the seven (7) infrastructure projects transferred by the DPWH with aggregate cost of ₱111,053,318.10 to come up with a reliable and accurate financial information. The said properties acquired through non-exchange transaction (transfer at no consideration) shall be measured at its fair value pursuant to pertinent provisions of IPSAS 17.

We recommended to the Management the following:
Direct the Municipal Agriculturist to provide the Municipal Accountant a complete inventory of biological assets, including full details on age, physical transformation, prevailing market prices, and cost to sell as of the reporting date, in compliance with Item 4.5 of COA Circular No. 2016-004 dated September 30, 2016;
Municipal Direct the Municipal Accountant to recognize the four (4) newborn kids pursuant to Paragraph 16 of IPSAS 27; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure compliance with the minimum disclosure requirements of IPSAS 27 – Agriculture regarding the Biological Assets account for fair presentation.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant immediately conduct a reconciliation of the account balance presented in the financial statements and the supporting schedules and prepare the necessary adjustments to correct the discrepancy and come up with an accurate and reliable financial information.

We recommended that Management:
Cause the immediate return to the source agencies the unexpended balances of completed projects and activities totaling to ₱389,700.00 to fully settle the obligations; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to establish and maintain separate subsidiary ledgers for each source agency in compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 94-013 to ensure proper monitoring, accountability, and reporting of fund transfers.

1.	Require the Municipal Budget Officer to maintain separate Registry of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations (RAAO) and subsidiary records of DRRM Fund in compliance with Section 5.1.4 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012; and
2.	Require the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer to prepare and submit the monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRM Fund in compliance with Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to secure the necessary documents to facilitate the payment and release of the retention monies amounting to ₱214,624.19 pursuant to pertinent provisions of Section 62 of the 2016 RIRR of R.A. No. 9184. Any amount due to the LGU by the concerned suppliers/contractors shall be deducted therefrom; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to submit a written explanation why he has not deducted retention monies from the payments of procured goods thru public bidding nor required the submission of equivalent special bank guarantee by the suppliers in accordance with Section 62 of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of R.A. No. 9184.
We recommended that Management continuously ensure that all withheld taxes are remitted on time in compliance with Republic Act No. 8424, as amended by R.A. No. 10963 and other BIR Revenue Issuances.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to submit their written explanation on the failure to withhold taxes in the aggregate amount of ₱21,323.13. Moreover, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the said withholding agents to ensure proper withholding and full remittance of taxes pursuant to pertinent BIR regulations.

We recommended that Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to consistently comply with Section 6 of Republic Act No. 8291, particularly on the full and timely withholding and remittance of GSIS premium contributions and loan amortizations.

We recommended that Management continue to strictly comply with Sections 11 and 18 of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations (RIRR) of Republic Act No. 7875, as amended, specifically on the full and timely withholding and remittance of premium contributions.

We recommended to Management the following:
The Management to submit justification on the lack of enactment by the Sanggunian in determining the amount to be granted as SRI;
The Management to stop the practice of transferring funds between the Executive and Legislative departments through budget augmentations; and
The Municipal Budget Officer to use the prescribed augmentation form (LBE Form No. 2) provided in the Budget Operations Manual (BOM) for LGUs, 2023 edition.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management direct the concerned personnel in the Municipal Treasury Office to prepare the RCI using the prescribed format and instructions in Form No. 47 of NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume II.

We recommended to Management the following:
1.		Require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to prepare the approved Annual Procurement Plan (APP) following the prescribed format in GPPB Circular 07-2015 dated July 16, 2015;
2.		Attach the authenticated photocopy of the approved APP in every submitted contract and/or Disbursement Voucher on procurement made through Public Bidding pursuant to Section 9.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012; and
3.		Attach the Approved Procurement Plan in every submitted Disbursement Voucher for Procurement made through Alternative mode pursuant to Section 9.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

We recommended that Management direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and the Municipal Accountant to submit a written explanation on the thirteen (13) unsubmitted contracts totaling to ₱32,231,514.98 as well as on the delays incurred in the submission of contracts and their supporting documents related to the procurement of twenty-seven (27) infrastructure projects totaling to ₱58,295,988.65.

We recommended to Management the following:
Direct the Bids and Awards Committee to ensure compliance with all applicable provisions of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations (RIRR) of Republic Act No. 9184 pertaining to the Small Value Procurement modality; and
Require the Municipal Accountant to submit the lacking documents on the identified procurement activities to forestall audit suspension of the covered transactions.

We recommended that Management submit written explanation stating the reasons which contributed to the non-implementation of the projects and the “catch-up” program of the LGU to henceforth ensure that the programs, projects and activities under the 20% Development Fund are fully implemented during the year in line with Section 3.2.3 of DBM-DOF-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 1 dated November 4, 2020.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to cause the actual cash transfer at the end of the year of the unexpended balance of the QRF and DRRM-MOOE to a Special Trust Fund in compliance with the pertinent provisions of Republic Act No. 10121. Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that the LDDRM Fund is properly monitored and that the accounting and reporting guidelines on the unspent balance of the LDRRMF as prescribed in Sections 5.1.10 and 5.1.13 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 are fully complied with.

We recommended that Management require the GAD Focal Person to submit the GAD Plan and Budget, along with the Accomplishment Reports and their supporting documents, and strictly observe the period prescribed under Item V of COA Circular No. 2014-001 dated March 18, 2024, to ensure the timely evaluation by the Audit Team of these reports.

We recommended that the Management:
Direct the LSB to submit the SEF Budget Accountability Form No. 1 on SEF Utilization, as prepared by the Municipal Accountant, to the DepEd - Central Office, through appropriate channels, copy furnish other oversight bodies such as the Regional Offices of DBM and DILG on or before the 20th after the end of the quarter for the quarterly reports and on or before the 15th day of February of the following year for the annual reports; and
Instruct the LSB to review and approve the SEF Budget not later than October 31 of the current year, and furnish a copy thereof to the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Budget Officer, and Municipal Treasurer not later than seven (7) days after approval, in compliance with the indicative timelines specified in Item 6.1 of DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017, dated January 19, 2017.

We recommended that the Municipal Vice Mayor and the Sangguniang Bayan stop the practice of granting financial assistance to the constituents as it is not among the functions of the legislative department in view of Sections 445 and 447 of R.A. No. 7160. Moreover, we recommended that funds intended for the granting of financial assistance, aids or donations be henceforth appropriated by the Sangguniang Bayan and obligated by the Municipal Budget Officer under the Donations account instead of the Other MOOE account in line with Section 2.2.18 of DBM Local Budget Memorandum 85 dated June 15, 2022 and in compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.

We recommended that Management formulate a comprehensive and well-defined policy and guidelines in the granting of donations and financial assistance that would ensure equitable allocation of funds among the constituents, enable the LGU to systematically provide assistance to more beneficiaries, ensure uniform and complete documentation of disbursement of government funds, and safeguard the government resources against loss or wastage in line with Section 2 of P.D. No. 1445.

We recommended that Management direct the Petty Cash Custodian (PCC) to prepare Petty Cash Vouchers (PCVs) to document the payments or disbursements from the Petty Cash, refrain from paying financial assistance from the Petty Cash, and to henceforth ensure that the fund shall only be used to non-regular, non-recurring, emergency and petty operating expenses of the LGU in compliance with COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 and Section 48 of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the Management:
Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to observe the instructions provided in the abovementioned regulation with regard to the orderly and systematic issuance of accountable forms; and
Stop the practice of rotating collectors between different collecting units. Instead, assign each collector to a single, specific collecting unit to ensure consistency and accountability in their work. Alternatively, formulate and implement clear internal controls or procedures requiring the use of separate booklets for each type of collection. These procedures should be designed to prevent confusion among the collectors, ensure proper sequential issuance of receipts, and promote accuracy in financial documentation. Regular monitoring and training on these controls will help strengthen compliance and reduce the risk of errors.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer prepare a Requisition and Issue Slip whenever an accountable form was issued. Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer implement stronger procedures for tracking, documenting and reconciling accountable forms to prevent future lapses.

We recommended that the Management through the Municipal Treasurer:
1.	Ensure that petty cash fund is maintained and handled by the designated Accountable Officer (Petty Cash Custodian) in adherence to Section 4.1.6 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997; and
2.	Stop the practice of transferring the petty cash and other cash advances to unbonded employees of the LGU and limit the assignment of essential and regular functions, especially those requiring accountability for government funds, only to bonded permanent employees in order to safeguard public funds and protect the interest of the government.

We recommended that the Management require the duly designated Petty Cash Custodians to ensure that the Petty Cash Vouchers are first approved prior to releasing the payments in strict adherence to Section 48 of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to submit to the COA the RCD with the duplicate ORs and validated deposit slips to facilitate proper reconciliation of collections and deposits and ensure transparency and accountability in the financial reporting process.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer require the collectors in the Municipal Treasury Office to religiously observe prompt remittance of their collections. Moreover, we recommended that the Liquidating Officer strictly verify the collections and the accountable forms pursuant to Section 30, Chapter 3, Volume I of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for LGUs to prevent misappropriation or unauthorized use of collections in the custody of the Collecting Officers.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer ensure that monthly bank statements or snapshots are regularly and timely furnished to the Municipal Accountant and the Audit Team for its prompt evaluation. Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare and submit the Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS) to ensure that cash balances in the depository accounts of the municipality are properly recorded and monitored.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
Provide adequate information on the other parcels of Land which were presented in the financial statements such as the acquisition cost of the Municipal Building Lot and the two parcels of Municipal Plaza; and
Require the Municipal Accountant to use the cost model in the recognition and subsequent measurement of PPEs and prepare the necessary Adjusting Journal Entry (AJE) to correct the balance of Land account in compliance with IPSAS 17 and its Philippine Application Guidance to come up with a more accurate and reliable financial information.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary Adjusting Journal Entries (AJE) to drop the non-capitalizable PPEs with aggregate cost of ₱1.260 million to correct the balance of the PPE account reported in the financial statements in line with Section 27 of IPSAS 1 and Section 111 of P.D. 1445 to come up with a more accurate and reliable information and to replace the existing Acknowledgment Receipt for Equipment (ARE) or Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR) by ICS for high-value items.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant derecognize the PPE items which are below the capitalization threshold and rectify the misclassification of accounts noted in our audit. Thereafter, recompute the depreciation of PPE items and prepare the necessary Adjusting Journal Entry (AJE) to correct the reported balance of the appropriate Accumulated Depreciation and Prior Period Adjustment/Government Equity accounts in line with Section 27 of IPSAS 1 and Section 111 of P.D 1445.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary Adjusting Journal Entry (AJE) to correct the misclassification of accounts totaling to ₱16.614 million in compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 to come up with a more accurate and reliable financial information.

We recommended that Management consider transferring the PPEs totaling to ₱34.916 million to the recipient government offices by accomplishing the Property Transfer Receipts (PTRs) which will be the basis of Municipal Accountant in preparing journal entries to drop the items of PPE from the books of accounts of LGU San Mateo, Isabela and come up with a more accurate and reliable financial information.

We recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to recognize the donated PPEs in the accounting books upon securing the necessary documents from the respective donors to establish the cost of the donated items, otherwise assign cost based on the fair market value of the assets in compliance with IPSAS 24 and COA Circular No. 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary Adjusting Journal Entry (AJE) to correct the misclassification of account totaling to ₱25.620 million in compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 to come up with a more accurate and reliable financial information.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal General Service Officer prepare and maintain the prescribed forms, registries and reports mentioned in COA Circular No. 2022-004 to strengthen controls over the semi-expendable properties and to categorize the semi-expendable properties to low-valued or high-valued items.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to stop the practice of recording purchases as outright expense and apply the perpetual inventory method in recording purchases. Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal GSO to prepare the necessary records and reports such as the SLC, SC, SSMI and RIS in accordance with pertinent provisions of the NGAS Manual for LGUs.

We recommended that Management install strong internal control procedures that will ensure proper handling and accounting of supplies/materials to minimize the risk of loss, wastage, and misuse in view of Sections 123 and 124 of P.D. 1445.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to use the cost model in the recognition and subsequent measurement of Investment Property assets and prepare the necessary Adjusting Journal Entry (AJE) to correct the reported balance of the Investment Property-Land account in compliance with IPSAS 16 and its corresponding Philippine Application Guidance to come up with a more accurate and reliable financial information.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary Adjusting Journal Entry (AJE) to correct the misclassification of accounts in compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 and prepare the necessary supporting schedules such as the Subsidiary Ledgers and ageing schedules on a periodic basis to facilitate monitoring and evaluation of the account in line with Sections 111 and 114(2) of P.D. 1445.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare adjusting journal entries to reclassify the Goodwill Fee- Market balance of ₱6,597,549.78 and Goodwill Fee- Pasalubong Center balance of ₱628,042.50 as occupancy fee under the Customers’ Deposits account in compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 to come with a more accurate and reliable financial information.

Thereafter, we recommended that certain portion of the collected occupancy fees for the San Mateo Commercial Center and San Mateo Pasalubong Center be recognized as Rent Income under the General Fund following the guidelines prescribed in Ordinance Nos. 2022-871 and 2019-787.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting journal entry to correct the misclassification of Finance Lease Receivable amounting to ₱16,614,214.65 and thereafter recognize allowance for impairment on the said account in compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 to come up with a more accurate and reliable financial information. Moreover, we recommended that Management issue Demand Letters to the concerned individuals and exhaust all possible remedies to collect the receivable balance of ₱2,438,558.00 which could have been used in the implementation of the programs, projects and activities of the LGU.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary Adjusting Journal Entry (AJE) to correct the misclassification of accounts in line with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 to come up with a more accurate and reliable financial information.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary Adjusting Journal Entry (AJE) to correct the misclassification of account in compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 to come up with a more accurate and reliable financial information. Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary supporting schedules on a periodic basis such as the ageing report to facilitate monitoring and evaluation of the account in line with Sections 111 and 114(2) of P.D. 1445.

We recommend to Management the following:
Direct the Municipal Treasurer to prepare and update its Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s Index Card based on the database and assessment roll of the Municipal Assessor and thereafter forward a copy of certified list of the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year to the Municipal Accountant as the basis in setting-up the Real Property Tax Receivable and Special Education Tax Receivable; and
Require the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to evaluate and analyze the discrepancies between the tax delinquencies of RPT and SET reported in the financial statements as of the year ending December 31, 2023 and the Certified List of All Property Tax Delinquencies. Thereafter, effect the necessary adjustments to come up with a more accurate and reliable financial information in view of Section 27 of International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS 1) and Section 111 of P.D. 1445.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to immediately reconcile the account balances presented in the Trial Balance and Subsidiary Ledgers, immediately settle the intra-agency transactions to avoid accumulation of unreconciled balances and eliminate the account balances in the consolidated financial statements to come up with a more reliable and accurate financial information in line with Paragraph 40(c) of IPSAS 35 and Paragraph 27 of IPSAS 1.

We recommended that Management ensure the timely and full implementation of the projects, cause the immediate liquidation of the funds transferred by other local government units and return the unexpended balances to the source agencies upon completion of the projects to fully settle the obligations. Moreover, we recommended that Management comply with Section 33 of R.A. 7160 by executing Memoranda of Agreement with the concerned barangays in relation to the implementation of counterpart projects. Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that the transfers of funds are recorded in both the books of the LGU and the barangays to establish accountability and responsibility in line with COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994 and prepare the necessary supporting schedules such as the ageing report and Subsidiary Ledgers to facilitate monitoring and evaluation of accounts.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to exert efforts in identifying the particular transactions from which the retention monies were deducted and send notice to the concerned creditors to submit their claims, otherwise revert the amount of ₱298,691.00 which remained unclaimed for more than two years to the Government Equity account in line with Section 98 of PD No. 1445.

We recommended to Management the following:
Issue Demand Letters to the concerned individuals and exhaust all possible remedies to collect the Accounts Receivable balance of ₱5,052,785.50 which could have been used in the implementation of the programs, projects and activities of the LGU; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare Subsidiary Ledgers for each customer and ageing schedule, and thereupon recognize allowance for impairment on the Accounts Receivable balance pursuant to COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 to come up with a more accurate and reliable financial information.

We recommended to the Management the following:
Identify the cause(s) of the deficiency and immediately coordinate with the PhilHealth to reconcile and update agency/employee records; and
Ensure full and timely deduction and remittance of PhilHealth premium contributions of employees in strict adherence to Paragraph 2 of PhilHealth Circular No. 004-2015.

We recommended to the Municipal Accountant the following:
Identify the cause(s) of the deficiency and immediately coordinate with the GSIS to reconcile and update agency/employee records;
Require the Agency Authorized Officer (AAO), Electronic Remittance File (ERF) Officer and the Municipal Accountant to strictly observe the full and proper deduction and remittance of GSIS premium contributions of employees in compliance with Section 6 of R.A. No. 8291; and
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entries to come up with a more accurate and reliable financial information.

Likewise, we recommended that the MDRRMO, in a separate caption, include in the subsequent LDRRMFIP and in the revised Annual Investment Plan (AIP) the unexpended balance of the QRF and DRRM-MOOE for CYs 2019-2022 amounting to ₱4,437,108.86, along with any additional savings from all completed projects, to ensure its appropriate release and utilization.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to revisit its existing practices and formulate new strategies to improve its collection efficiency rate and performance in line with DOF Department Order No. 006-2015 dated January 20, 2015 and BLGF Unnumbered Memorandum dated February 1, 2023.

We recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to maintain special accounts in the General Fund for the Municipality’s local economic enterprises and public utilities by preparing a complete set of subsidiary ledgers for each special account and by submitting the corresponding financial statements to form part of the schedules of the General Fund trial balance as required under pertinent Sections of the NGAS Manual, Volume I. Moreover, we recommended that Management review and assess the operations of the LEEs/PUs and institute appropriate measures that would result in improved financial performance and self-sufficiency of the LEEs/PUs.

Considering that the features of the existing accounting software is no longer compliant with the policy changes introduced by the Commission on Audit in the NGAS Manual for LGUs and/or the GAM for LGUs, we recommended that Management avail the eNGAS and eBudget System developed and prescribed by COA as the accounting and budgeting software for the government. In the meantime, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain a parallel manual recording and reporting of transactions to ensure strict compliance with the accounting policies, accounting books/record/forms and reports, and the Revised Chart of Accounts prescribed by the applicable COA Manuals. Also, we recommended that Management provide the Audit Team with access to the existing systems used in the different offices such as accounting, treasury, budget, etc. in line with COA Circular No. 2020-010 dated December 2, 2020.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer deposit intact all his collections, as well as all collections turned over to him by the collectors/tellers, with the authorized depository bank daily or not later than the next banking day in compliance with Section 32, Chapter 3, Volume I of the MNGAS for LGUs to avoid accumulation of huge amount of cash in his custody. Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer strengthen the internal control in the safekeeping of collections to avoid losses of government funds in view of the Internal Control Standards for the Philippine Public Sector.

We recommended that the Management refrain from granting cash advances/petty cash fund to elected officials other than for purposes of travelling expenses pursuant to Section 339 of R.A. No. 7160 and Section 4.1.4 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

We recommended that the Management thru the Municipal Accountant monitor and issue demand letters to the concerned officials and employees to fully liquidate their cash advances, otherwise withhold the salaries of the erring employees, if warranted, as provided under COA Circular 2012-004 dated November 28, 2012.

We recommended that the Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant to submit the lacking supporting documents for the cash advances for Confidential Fund set forth in the COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 08, 2015; and
Consider increasing the amount of fidelity bond of the Special Disbursing Officer in accordance with Bureau of Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 dated August 6, 2009 to comply with the bonding requirement.

We recommended that Management ensure the timely and full implementation of the projects, cause the immediate liquidation of the funds transferred and return the unexpended balances to the source agencies upon completion of the projects to fully settle the obligations.

We recommended to Management and the Local School Board (LSB) the following:
Regularly monitor and evaluate the balance of the SEF and ensure inclusion of the unexpended balances in the SEF budget for the ensuing year to maximize its utilization in order to benefit the students and schools and enable them to implement other learning activities; and
Give equal priority to the implementation of the construction, repair, and maintenance of school buildings and facilities to benefit the students and ensure that the SEF be allocated only to programs, projects, and activities which are enumerated in DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1 series 2017, as revised.

We recommended that the Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to submit written explanation as to why the Disbursement Vouchers and its supporting documents were consistently submitted late;
Require the Network Administrator/IT personnel to address the system errors/glitches and/or coordinate with the provider/supplier of the accounting system to address the problems noted and henceforth ensure to timely resolve similar issues so as not to cause disruption in the recording of transactions; and
Strictly comply with the guidelines in the timely submission of financial reports, in adherence with Section 7.2.1 of COA Circular 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009, to facilitate prompt audit of agency transactions and balances.

We recommended that Management direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to immediately submit copies of all unsubmitted contracts together with its supporting documents and ensure that copies of contracts are submitted within the prescribed period of five (5) days from issuance thereof in strict adherence to item 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009. Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant immediately submit the corresponding Disbursement Vouchers and its supporting documents to the Audit Team, otherwise the transactions covered by the unsubmitted documents may be suspended in audit.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) prepare the Annual Procurement Plan (APP) following the format prescribed in GPPB Circular 07-2015 and attach the authenticated photocopy of the approved APP in every submitted contract pursuant to Section 9.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 and pertinent provisions of Section 7 of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of R.A. No. 9184.

Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Accountant prepare and attach to the progress/final billings the additional documentary requirements common to all infrastructure transactions in accordance with Section 9.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 to avoid the issuance of audit suspensions.

We recommended that Management:
Require the concerned contractors to immediately repair, rectify or correct the deficiencies noted in the inspection and conform with the approved program of works otherwise, require them to refund the equivalent amount of ₱2,790,834.85 to prevent issuance of Notice of Disallowance (ND) by the COA Audit Team;
Create an Inspectorate Team that is tasked among others to conduct preliminary inspection once the project reaches an accomplishment of ninety-five (95%) of the total contract amount and submit punch-list of the remaining works, work deficiencies for necessary corrections, and the specific duration/time to fully complete the project considering the approved remaining contract time; and
Direct the Municipal Engineer and the Inspectorate Team to henceforth conduct regular and strict monitoring and supervision of all LGU implemented infrastructure projects to ensure that these are accomplished in accordance with the approved program of works and completed within their specified contract time to fully achieve the intended benefits of these projects.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal Engineer to check and certify the amounts in the submitted Statement of Work Accomplished (SWA) of the contractors in compliance to Section 5, Annex E of the 2016 RIRR of R.A. No. 9184;
Require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to supply the needed information as to the contract duration of the identified projects by immediately submitting the corresponding Invitation to Bid (ITB), the Bid and Award Notice Abstracts, in view of Section 21.1.h of the 2016 RIRR of R.A. No. 9184; and
Ensure that the date of the Notice to Proceed (NTP) should be indicated and require the winning bidder/s the acknowledgement/receipt of the NTP because such date will serve as the effectivity of the contract pursuant to Section 37.4.1 of the 2016 RIRR of R.A. No. 9184.

We recommended to Management the following:
Direct the GSO and the members of the Inspectorate Team to submit written explanation why the rotavator was accepted despite non-conformity with the LGU’s required specifications. Determine if there is difference between the prices of the delivered and ordered items, and if the delivered item is determined to be cheaper, require the supplier to refund the excess amount;
Direct the GSO and the members of the Inspectorate Team to submit written justification why the Acceptance and Inspection Report comes first before the delivery date of the Manlift Truck; and
Henceforth strictly observe the guidelines in the conduct of acceptance and inspection of the items delivered.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer observe Sections 101 and 102 of Presidential Decree No. 1445 by installing adequate internal control measures in the Municipal Treasury Office (MTO) that would ensure that relevant policies and procedures in the proper handling of the funds of the Municipality are strictly implemented and monitored to avoid incurrence of cash shortage/s in the future.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to conduct regular reconciliation of its records and reports and effect the necessary adjustments to ensure effective monitoring of accountabilities and present a more accurate and reliable financial information.
We recommended the Municipal Treasurer to observe the instructions provided in the abovementioned regulation with regard to the orderly and systematic issuance of accountable forms.

We recommended that Management direct the concerned employees to secure adequate bonding with the Bureau of Treasury to ensure that the LGU is duly and adequately indemnified in the event of defalcations, shortages and losses of funds under their custody.

We recommended that the LGU strengthen its internal control in the safekeeping of funds and procure/install a safe/vault where the Liquidating Officer may keep the collections pending its deposit to the bank.

We recommended that Management update the Treasury Income Monitoring System being used by the Municipal Treasury Office in recording its transactions specifically the daily collections in accordance with the accrual basis of accounting prescribed in IPSAS 1 to ensure fair presentation of financial statements and transactions, and effectively monitor accountabilities.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer prepare and maintain the cashbooks and supply the required information therein in accordance with Annexes 19-21 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) for Local Government Units, Volume II.

We recommended that Management:
Observe the principle of segregation of duties in designating Petty Cash Fund Custodians (PCFCs) to ensure that checks and balances exist in the granting, utilization, and liquidation of cash advances in line with the Internal Control Standards for the Philippine Public Sector (ICSPPS); and
Direct the Petty Cash Fund Custodians (PCFCs) to ensure that the Petty Cash Fund shall only be used for the non-regular, non-recurring, emergency and petty expenses of the LGU in compliance with Item 4.3.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to recognize interest expenses and applicable charges imposed on its loans upon incurrence and ensure strict compliance with the accrual basis of accounting.

Current portion of the Loans Payable account with an outstanding balance of ₱237,520,891.50 was not recognized in the books contrary to Items 70 and 80 of IPSAS 1, resulting to doubtful fair presentation of the Expense and Liability accounts as at year-end.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to determine and recognize the current portion of the Loans Payable in conformity to IPSAS 1.

Contracts of the respective loans were not provided contrary to Section 59 of Presidential Decree 1445 and Item 27 of International Public Sector Accounting Standard (IPSAS) 1, thus depriving the conduct of more comprehensive auditing and accurate account disclosure.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive to instruct the concerned government official/employee in charge of the custody of the Loan Agreements/Contracts to furnish the same to the Office of the Auditor. The Municipal Accountant must also sufficiently provide fair presentation and disclosure of the loan payable account.

observations.recommendation:
We strongly reiterated our previous recommendations that the Municipal Mayor
direct the:

a. Inventory Committee and the GSO to conduct a complete physical inventory of
all the Municipality’s property and correspondingly prepare and submit to the Audit
Team the required inventory report thereon;

b. Municipal Accountant and GSO to reconcile their reports and investigate
discrepancies noted between the inventory report and accounting and property
records; and

c. Municipal Accountant and GSO to make the necessary adjustments to reconcile
the PPE accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. continue exerting efforts in tracing back prior years’ acquisition of Property,
Plantand Equipment to have a complete details of the Infrastructure Assets account
totaling P154,355,688.96 so that the corresponding depreciation could be
computed, to present the true value of the said PPE account;

b. draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to adjust the understatement of the
Depreciation Expense and Accumulated Depreciation accounts in the amount of
R681,504.26 of the affected PPE accounts; and

c. henceforth, strictly abide with PAG 3 of IPSAS 17.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
make an adjusting entry by debiting the Land account and crediting Other
Investments account by P591,600.00, to present the true value of the affected
accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
continue exerting efforts to locate and retrieve the relevant documents pertaining
to the long outstanding receivable accounts to establish its validity and legality.
For those receivables proven to be valid, the Management should exhaust all
possible means to enforce payment from the debtors. Otherwise, the Municipal
Mayor should request the write-off of the dormant receivable accounts to the
Commission on Audit, duly supported with the documentary requirements
prescribed in Item 8.3 of COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We strongly reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the
Municipal Accountant to:

a. coordinate with the Provincial Accountant for the reconciliation of Due from and
Due to LGUs accounts so that appropriate adjustments could be made on the
affected accounts for fair presentation in the Municipality’s financial statements;
and

b. henceforth, conduct periodic reconciliation of Municipal accounts with its
reciprocal accounts in the Province's books to avoid the accumulation of
discrepancies between two records.

We recommended that Municipal Mayor to direct the Municipal Treasurer to:

a. immediately remit to the BIR the amount of P790,852.94, which pertains to the
unremitted premium contributions consisting both of the Municipality’s and
employees’ shares and loan payments; and

b. henceforth, strictly comply with Sections 6(b) and 7 of RA No. 8291.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. analyze and determine the payables without valid documentation and those
that have been outstanding for two years or more, and subsequently request an
authority to the Sangguniang Bayan for the reversion of the same; and

b. prepare the necessary journal entry to revert the said outstanding obligations
to Government Equity pursuant to Section 98 of PD No. 1445 so that the Municipality
can use the available funds for other priority projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and
Municipal Treasurer to:

a. immediately remit the tax withheld amounting to P132,303.35 from
officers/employees, contractors/suppliers and other entities to the BIR; and

b. Henceforth, ensure prompt and full remittance of taxes withheld pursuant to
Section 2.58 of BIR Revenue Regulations No. 2-98, to avoid incurrence of interest
as defined under Section 11 of BIR Revenue Regulations No. 11-2018.

We recommended that Municipal Mayor to direct the Municipal Treasurer:

a. to immediately remit the amount of £74,425.08 pertaining to unremitted
premium contributions consisting both of the Municipality’s and employees’ shares; and

b. henceforth, strictly comply with Section 20(b), Rule III, Title III of the Revised
IRR of the National Health Insurance Act.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. continuously exert effort to gather the needed documents and draw journal
entry vouchers to record all valid reconciling items that require adjustments and
correction in the books for fair presentation of the balance of affected accounts in
the financial statements; and

b. henceforth, strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 96-011
dated October 2,1996.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. ensure that all financial reports and their supporting documents are submitted to
the Audit Team within the prescribed period as required under COA Circular Nos.
95-006 and 2009-006. Otherwise, any unjustified failure on the part of the official
concerned to submit the required reports shall be considered a ground for the
automatic suspension of payment of his salaries until he shall have complied
therewith without prejudice to any disciplinary action that may be instituted
against him, pursuant to Section 122 of PD No. 1445 (The Government Auditing
Code of the Philippines); and

b. determine the cause/s of the delay in the submission of the accounts of the
Municipality to the Audit Team and find solutions thereto.

We recommended that Management shall immediately return unused/unutilized balances of completed projects to the Source Agencies and/or remit to the National Treasury as required under COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994.

We recommended that Management must exert efforts to collect amounts due from the Bliss Dwellers (Housing Units) located at Barangay San Marcos, San Mateo by sending Demand Letters (DLs) specifying the period to settle overdue accounts. In addition, stricter collection scheme must be enforced to avoid delinquent payors which may put the Municipality at a disadvantage.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to:
Require the Municipal Agriculturist to submit Inventory of Biological Assets and provide the prevailing market price for each item as of reporting date as well as the prevailing cost to sell of the biological assets; and
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to maintain Work, Other Animals & Breeding Stocks Ledger Card (WOABSLC) and see to it that it is reconciled with the Property Cards which must be maintained as well by the General Services Officer.
We recommended management to strictly adhere with the prescribed period of remittances to the proper recipient government agencies in compliance with the applicable laws and rules.

We recommended Management to:
Limit the use of Petty Cash Fund for non-recurring, emergency and petty expenses;
Require the Petty Cash Custodian to prepare Petty Cash Voucher duly attached to all required supporting documents for each petty operating expenses; and
Strictly comply with the applicable rules and regulations under PD 1445, COA Circular No. 97-002, COA Circular No. 2012-001 and the NGAS Manual for LGUs.

We recommended the Municipal Treasurer to:
Deposit all collections promptly and intact daily; if not, as regular as possible and avoid keeping of substantial amount of collections especially on Fridays; and
Strictly observe the rules and regulations cited under Section 69 (1) of PD No. 1445, Section 2 of COA Circular No. 97-002, Section 32 of the MNGAS for LGUs, and Revised Cash Examination Manual.
We recommended that Management strictly adhere to the funding requirement of both the 5% Calamity Fund and economic enterprises as set forth in the above-mentioned laws, rules and regulations.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting entries relative to the misclassified/misstated accounts;
Instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer to reconcile their reports to establish the correct balances and the Municipal Treasurer must immediately transfer the cash back-up of the special trust fund (QRF & MOOE-Mitigation Fund) to the trust fund account; and
Strictly and prudently comply with all the rules and regulations laid out relative to LDRRM Fund.

We recommended that Management provide justification on the utilization of 20% Economic Development pertaining to the aforementioned transaction. Moreover, require the concerned official or employee to submit all necessary documents to support the distribution and implementation of the project.

We recommended that Management:
Require the concerned officials to submit original copies of proofs of distribution, with identification of the type of relief goods, duly signed by actual beneficiaries for our evaluation. Likewise, provide an explanation for the uneven distribution of family food packs to individuals as observed on the attachment of Check No. 82823 dated July 21, 2021;
Submit legal basis in granting meal allowances to employees; and
Enjoin all concerned officials to strictly comply with the above-stated law, rules and regulations.

We recommended that Management submit legal basis for the increase in the Pag-IBIG government compulsory contribution that which requires the proper consultation and approval of DBM as required under RA 11518 and RA 9679. The management should strictly observe the maximum limit of PAG-IBIG government compulsory contribution at P100.00 per month per official/employee unless the LGU consult with and secure approval of DBM.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to enjoin the Inventory Committee and all concerned offices to:
Undertake the inventory taking in accordance with the procedures set in the new Guidelines; and
Reconcile and update their records and reports accordingly.

We recommended that Management must comply with the rule that payments shall be made by checks directly payable to the suppliers and streamlined process of procurement mandated in Republic Act No. 9184 must be observed.

We recommended Management to create one (1) additional mandatory DRRM position for technical staff to complement the LDRRM Office and fill up the same in compliance with the provision of Section 12 of RA 10121 to achieve the setting of the direction, development, implementation and coordination of disaster risk management program
We recommended that Management must submit the above-cited lacking documents as required in Item 5.16 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 for our review and evaluation.

We recommended that the concerned officials shall ensure that projects, programs and activities appropriated from the 20% Development Fund are specifically identified in the AIP describing thereat in detail the project component and project site/location pursuant to COA MC No. 97-004 and Sangguniang Panlalawigan review.

We recommended that Management shall enjoin the concerned officials to strictly comply with the reportorial requirements laid out in COA Circular No. 2012-002 to have a more reliable and accurate monitoring report for 5% LDRRM Fund utilization and balances.

We recommended that Management shall fully comply with the posting requirements laid-out in the above-mentioned law, rules and regulations in order to fully promote transparency, accountability, and good governance policies and measures in the procurement process.

We recommended that Management must observe strict compliance with the reportorial and procedural guidelines for confidential expenses set forth in COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015.

We recommended that management:
Submit a written explanation why the local government unit has not yet adopted the ATM Payroll system considering its advantages; and
Establish and adopt the ATM payroll system effective CY 2022 and onwards, in the payment of benefits to regular and casual employees as well as salaries and wages to JOs and COSs in conformity with the DBM NPS to ensure greater efficiency, transparency and accountability in the government’s human resource processes.

We recommended that the GAD Focal Person submit timely to the Audit Team the GAD Accomplishment Report with the required supporting documents cited in PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 and pursuant to Item V of COA Circular No. 2014-001.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the concerned officials to satisfactorily explain and justify the legality of granting honoraria to the committee members of TOCSMI.
observations.managementAction:
Management commented that the activity in issue is a special activity duly authorized thru a Municipal Ordinance. It was further explained that, the activity in issue do not fall within the ambit of or inherent to the regular functions of the Department to which the members belong and that the composition of the search committee embraces the involvement of different offices from the National and Local Governments, a clear showing that the task is merely incidental and inconsequential in the performance of the official duties of the members. To this, COA Decision No. 2008-126 do not apply to the present issue in that the decision pertain to activities inherent to the regular functions of the agency.

Further, Management commented that the honorarium as clearly defined in the internal rules is only five hundred pesos (Php 500.00) per session to defray the expenses in the conduct of background investigation of the different nominees. In reality, the members of the committee even resorted to use part of their personal money if only to fulfill their commitments, and assigned task. Also, the practice is a carryover from the two (2) previous administrations until this administration. It is obvious though that the past transactions of the same nature were not commented by the past auditors including the vey audit team leader who now issuing this AOM, and yet the regulation cited is dated way back in 2012.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management direct the subjected Contract of Service and Job Order personnel to cease the practice of signing and approving the Work Accomplishment Reports, Daily Time Records, payrolls and other document not within their authority; and require the department heads or at least the Unit/Section Chief to sign and approve the same.
observations.managementAction:
Management commented that when it was noted that Job Order employees are not clothed with authority to sign the Accomplishment Reports and Attendance sheets of fellow Job Order Workers, though assigned as the leaders of the group, upon notice of these, the practice was immediately stopped by management. Management squarely agree that records of attendance to work and accomplishment reports must be signed by authorized responsible officials of the hiring and paying agency. This regulation applies to all employees irrespective of status of appointments and in the case of Job Order employees of LGU San Mateo, their attendance to work and accomplishment reports must be signed by the LGU authorized officials including those detailed to National Offices like the office of the Audit Team Leader. The observed deficiency was a product of honest presumption of regularity based on the observation that the Audit Team Leader herself is the one signing the records of attendance and accomplishment reports of Ms. Syralyn Manibog, a Job Order employee of this LGU detailed to her office upon her request. It is also noted that the accomplishments in the report include performance of auditing works which constitute a breach to the well settled independence of the COA.

It was further explained that the Team's assertion under the 3 to the last paragraph of AOM No. 2020-18 exceeded the limit of auditing functions and even made personal views and interpretation on legal issues which are the subject of a case already filed with the court, acts which may prove offensive to the norms of conduct of auditors, and may even constitute subjudice.

Since mere stoppage of the assailed practice will not cure the noted defects of the transactions, these representations express their willingness to have the documents signed by the proper authorities, provided copies of these be furnished to the LGU to serve as basis of (sic) rectification.”

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management refrain from providing special services to the Consultant/Special Assistant for Legal and Fiscal Concerns and to the designated Special Assistant for Special Concerns.  Also, we recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to properly review completeness of the supporting documents of all transactions before forwarding to the Municipal Treasurer for payment.
observations.managementAction:
The documentary deficiencies were rectified. The functions assigned to the concerned personnel under these findings were already squarely aligned to their contract of employment. These are contained in their accomplishment reports.

Management commented that Mr. Michael Angelo Allas is a Job Order personnel under the Office of the Mayor Administrative Support Services holding the position of an Administrative Assistant (a generic position). The position implies flexibility because the description addresses expectations and accountabilities and doesn't get into the details of how task should be performed. Further explained that it is therefore expected for Mr. Michael Angelo Allas that he cannot rid of performing any function that are available under the Administrative Support Services of the Office of the Municipal Mayor. Serving as driver for the administrative services staff of the LCE at times during ocular inspections and recanvas, etc. are just incidental and will never be a reasonable basis to conclude that he is a personal driver of the consultants. His other accomplishments stand as mute but eloquent witness to this fact. It was likewise mentioned that the accomplishment report which contained that Mr. Michael Angelo Allas served as the driver of the Consultant/Special Assistant on Legal Fiscal and Other Concerns was only one of the functions being inherent of holding a generic function. The service of Mr. Allas was not only for the Consultant/Special Assistant on Legal Fiscal and Other Concerns but also for all the concerns of the OM-Administrative Support Services.

Further, Management commented that for the March 1 to 15, 2020 period to receive wages, this was the time that the President of the Republic of the Philippines declared a State of the Health Emergency. It was followed by lockdown of offices. So nobody was in the office except the employees who were considered front liners. This explains why there was no Accomplishment Report submitted and unsigned DTR.

As for Mr. Robinson Capulong, a Job Order employee with a designation as Traffic Aide under the POS, it was explained that based on his accomplishment report, he provided security for the Special Assistant on Special Concerns. It is worth mentioning that Atty. Larry Piedad was assigned a highly confidential concern which place his safety to high risk as in the case of Mr. Napoleon Hernandez, the supposed Municipal Administrator who was killed in an ambush. The assignment of Mr. Capulong was only for a limited time. He is now back to his post as Traffic Aide. The management acknowledged the wisdom of your observation and that Mr. Capulong was now reassigned as Traffic Aide.

As to the issues concerning Face ID, the management explained that it has already addressed the issue by issuing a revised memorandum mandating all employees (Regular, Job Order, and Contract of Service) with some exceptions as provided in the said memorandum.
